coredata.dc:title: Interferometric investigation of nanofluid natural convection heat transfer: Addressing existing inconsistencies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.
coredata.dc:creator: Sahamifar, Soheil
Naylor, David
Friedman, Jacob
coredata.dc:description:
Despite extensive research on nanofluids, their widespread adoption remains limited due to conflicting findings, with optical studies reporting up to 75 % enhancement and heat balance methods showing less enhancement or even some reduction. This study employs Mach-Zehnder Interferometry (MZI) to investigate the heat transfer characteristics of Al2O3–water nanofluids in an inclined rectangular cavity heated from below, with an aspect ratio of 1.5 and an inclination angle of 9.3°. The experiments were conducted under natural convection at Rayleigh numbers of 9.7
×
105 and 1.8
×
106, within the steady laminar flow regime. Leveraging the ability of MZI to simultaneously visualize and quantify temperature and concentration fields, the study aims to address possible reasons behind the inconsistencies reported in the literature. Three concentrations of Al2O3–water nanofluids provided by different preparation methods are examined: 0.05, 0.16, and 0.23 wt%. Their stability and thermal conductivity, both essential for accurate heat transfer measurements using MZI, are evaluated prior to the natural convection experiments. The optical path length of the experimental model is chosen to be short enough to ensure distinguishable fringes near the target surface, minimize the effects of surface refraction, reduce temperature measurement errors, and mitigate the impact of nanofluid instability. With the improvements made in this study, the results show that within the measurement uncertainty, the local Nusselt number distributions and average heat transfer rates for dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids with concentrations below 0.23 wt% (0.06 vol %) are the same as those of deionized water, regardless of the preparation method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural convection heat transfer
Al2O3–water nanofluid
Interferometry
Nusselt number
Thermal conductivity
Stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental model images: (a) Fully assembled model viewed along the beam direction; (b) middle fluid chamber section showing: (1) two Delrin side walls, (2) upper and lower aluminum plates, (3) fluid (water or nanofluid) cavity, (4) O-rings, (5) two thermocouples placed in designated holes to monitor temperature, and (6) fluid injection ports.

, after 1 h (steady state condition) for different case studies: (b) case c1, (c) case c2, (d) case c3.

10 6
, after 1 h (steady state condition) for different case studies: (b) case h1, (c) case h2, (d) case h3.

δ N u

head.ce:title: Interferometric investigation of nanofluid natural convection heat transfer: Addressing existing inconsistencies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical, Industrial, and Mechatronics Engineering, Toronto Metropolitan University, Toronto, Canada M5B 2K3

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical, Industrial, and Mechatronics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical, Industrial, and Mechatronics Engineering, Toronto Metropolitan University, Toronto, Canada M5B 2K3

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Despite extensive research on nanofluids, their widespread adoption remains limited due to conflicting findings, with optical studies reporting up to 75 % enhancement and heat balance methods showing less enhancement or even some reduction. This study employs Mach-Zehnder Interferometry (MZI) to investigate the heat transfer characteristics of Al2O3–water nanofluids in an inclined rectangular cavity heated from below, with an aspect ratio of 1.5 and an inclination angle of 9.3°. The experiments were conducted under natural convection at Rayleigh numbers of 9.7
×
105 and 1.8
×
106, within the steady laminar flow regime. Leveraging the ability of MZI to simultaneously visualize and quantify temperature and concentration fields, the study aims to address possible reasons behind the inconsistencies reported in the literature. Three concentrations of Al2O3–water nanofluids provided by different preparation methods are examined: 0.05, 0.16, and 0.23 wt%. Their stability and thermal conductivity, both essential for accurate heat transfer measurements using MZI, are evaluated prior to the natural convection experiments. The optical path length of the experimental model is chosen to be short enough to ensure distinguishable fringes near the target surface, minimize the effects of surface refraction, reduce temperature measurement errors, and mitigate the impact of nanofluid instability. With the improvements made in this study, the results show that within the measurement uncertainty, the local Nusselt number distributions and average heat transfer rates for dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids with concentrations below 0.23 wt% (0.06 vol %) are the same as those of deionized water, regardless of the preparation method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

Convective heat transfer coefficient (
W /
(
m 2
· K
)
)
Optical path length of the model in the beam direction (m)

ce:section.ce:para: Heat transfer enhancement has long been a primary focus for thermal designers seeking to improve thermal efficiency and reduce energy consumption. Techniques such as heat exchanger optimization, flow disruption using turbulators, and surface area extension with fins are commonly employed [1–3]. Over the past two decades, the utilization of nanofluids prepared by dispersing 1–100 nm nanoparticles into a base fluid has attracted considerable attention as a potential method for improving heat transfer performance [4,5]. However, despite extensive research, nanofluids have not yet seen widespread adoption in industrial and medical applications. This is partly due to inconsistencies reported across the literature [6,7]. In part to address these discrepancies, interferometry has been adopted by the authors of this study as a method to investigate the stability and thermophysical properties of nanofluids, as it enables detailed visualization of temperature and concentration fields [8–13]. The current research is an extension of previous works, focusing on the heat transfer characteristics of nanofluids by analyzing natural convection in transparent Al2O3–water nanofluids using an interferometric method.

Discrepancies in research findings on natural convection in nanofluids are evident, even within a specific nanofluid type such as Al2O3–water nanofluid. Ni et al. [14] reported a notable heat transfer reduction of approximately 15 % as nanoparticle concentration increased from 0 to 1.08 vol% in a turbulent Rayleigh–Benard configuration. Similarly, Kouloulias et al. [15], using a Rayleigh–Benard setup, observed a maximum deterioration of about 12 % at a nanoparticle concentration of 0.12 vol%. They attributed this primarily to nanoparticle sedimentation and the formation of an insulating nanoparticle layer on the heated surface, resulting from nanofluid instability. Furthermore, Putra et al. [16] and Li et al. [17] reported deterioration in natural convection heat transfer upon adding Al2O3 nanoparticles to the base fluid.

On the other hand, Ho et al. [18] found a different trend, observing that although higher Al2O3–water nanofluid concentrations (above 2 vol%) led to a decline in convective heat transfer, a significant enhancement of about 18 % was achieved at a low concentration of 0.1 vol% at the highest Rayleigh number of their study (
∼
10 8
). Similarly, Ghodsinezhad et al. [19] reported a peak enhancement in heat transfer of approximately 15 % at 0.1 vol% nanoparticle concentration, but further increases above this concentration resulted in reduced heat transfer performance. Moreover, Cioni et al. [20] and Nnanna [21] reported optimum Nusselt number enhancements at nanoparticle concentrations of 0.25 vol% and 1.25 vol%, respectively, beyond which it decreased.

In recent years, several optical studies on dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids with concentrations below 0.04 vol% have reported significant enhancements in heat transfer. Rao et al. [22] experimentally studied natural convection heat transfer around a vertical isothermal plate with a temperature difference of 1.2 °C relative to the surrounding Al2O3–water nanofluid at very dilute concentrations (0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 vol%). Their interferometric measurements showed a 21 % increase in convective heat transfer coefficient and a 14–15 % decrease in Nusselt number at 0.02 vol% concentration compared to pure water. Rao et al. [23], in another interferometric study, investigated heat transfer characteristics of dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids (0.01–0.04 vol%) in a cavity under Rayleigh–Benard convection with temperature differences of 1, 1.8, and 2.3 °C across the heated and cooled surfaces. They observed that both the convective heat transfer coefficient and the Nusselt number enhanced consistently with increasing nanoparticle concentration and temperature difference, achieving maximum enhancements of 38 % in the convective coefficient and 15 % in the Nusselt number at 0.04 vol% concentration. Jalan et al. [24] used Mach–Zehnder interferometry (MZI) and rainbow schlieren deflectometry to investigate natural convection around a heated horizontal cylinder in Al2O3–water nanofluids (0.005–0.02 vol%, ΔT = 0.5 and 1 °C). They observed that the heat transfer coefficient increased consistently with nanoparticle concentration, reaching a maximum enhancement of 45 % at 0.02 vol%, whereas the Nusselt number decreased up to 0.005 vol% and increased thereafter.

Rao et al., in two separate studies [25,26], employed MZI to obtain temperature fields from multiple view angles in cavities filled with Al2O3–water [25] and Al2O3-EGD (ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) [26] nanofluids. These interferometric measurements were subsequently used in tomographic reconstructions to visualize the three-dimensional thermal fields within the cavities. They found that increasing nanoparticle concentration and temperature difference (and therefore Rayleigh number) enhanced the Nusselt number, achieving maximum enhancements of 20 % for water-based and 32 % for EGD-based nanofluids. Jain et al. [27] employed rainbow schlieren deflectometry to investigate natural convection around an isothermal vertical plate immersed in Al2O3–water nanofluid (0.01, 0.02, and 0.03 vol%) at temperature differences of 1.5 and 2 °C. They reported that increasing nanoparticle concentration and temperature difference enhanced both convective and conductive heat transfer coefficients. The maximum convective coefficient enhancement of 75 % was observed at 0.03 vol% concentration.

As seen in the literature, conflicting findings have been reported regarding the impact of adding Al2O3 nanoparticles to water on natural convection heat transfer. Some studies [14–17] reported a reduction in heat transfer, while others [18–21] observed enhancement up to a certain concentration followed by deterioration when adding Al2O3 nanoparticles to water. On the other hand, some studies [22–27] reported significant improvement in heat transfer using very dilute concentrations. A closer look at existing studies shows that most [14,15,17,18,20–27] lacked a scientific approach to assess nanofluid stability, with many either not mentioning any method or relying solely on visual observations. However, the literature emphasizes that using a stable nanofluid is essential for nanofluid heat transfer studies [7,28,29], particularly in interferometric studies [8,9,12]. In our previous work [9], it was demonstrated that instability in the nanofluid can lead to significant optical temperature measurement errors, up to 100 % in conduction-dominated regions. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this is the first interferometric study on nanofluid natural convection in which the stability of the working nanofluid has been evaluated using scientific methods. Different methods, including the interferometric method proposed by the authors [8], zeta potential analysis, and nanoparticle size distribution are used to assess the stability of the working nanofluid.

The current work focuses on interferometric measurements. In interferometric studies, the local surface heat flux is calculated by multiplying the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid by the measured temperature gradient at the surface. This underscores the importance of accurately determining thermal conductivity. Since the thermal conductivity of nanofluids is influenced by several factors, such as the synthesis method, nanoparticle characteristics, additives, and pH levels [30], direct measurement of thermal conductivity is essential [11]. However, previous interferometric studies have relied on the Sharma correlation [31] to calculate thermal conductivity of their working nanofluid. This correlation was derived using data from nanofluids with different properties and operating conditions, which may not accurately represent the specific nanofluids used in previous studies [23–27]. Moreover, using the Sharma correlation [31], Rao et al. [23] reported approximately 37 % enhancement in the thermal conductivity of 0.01–0.04 vol% Al2O3–water nanofluid compared to water. Similarly, Jalan et al. [24] reported a thermal conductivity enhancement of 37–38 % for 0.005–0.02 vol%, and Jain et al. [27] found a 27 % increase for concentrations between 0.01 and 0.03 vol%. Based on our calculations using the Sharma correlation, the enhancement in thermal conductivity for concentrations of 0.04 vol% or lower is approximately 4 %, which often falls within the range of measurement uncertainty. The overestimation of thermal conductivity resulting from the inappropriate use of the Sharma correlation appears to be a significant contributing factor to the high heat transfer enhancements reported in previous optical studies [22–27]. To the authors’ knowledge, this is the first natural convection interferometric study in which the thermal conductivity of the working nanofluid has been experimentally measured as part of the study, rather than relying on empirical correlations.

Another important parameter for the optical measurement of nanofluid heat transfer is the temperature coefficient of refractive index (dn/dT) of the working fluid. Rao et al. [23,25] measured dn/dT for Al2O3–water nanofluids in the concentration range of 0.01–0.04 vol% using a refractometer. Their results showed that the dn/dT value at 0.01 vol% is almost the same as that of pure water. However, the absolute value of dn/dT decreased with increasing concentration, reaching nearly half at 0.04 vol%. This would significantly impact the measurements and contrasts with the findings of Yousefi et al. [32], who reported that dn/dT remained unchanged for Al2O3–water nanofluids at concentrations below 1 wt% (
∼
0.3 vol%). In our previous study [11], an interferometric technique was use and showed that for concentrations below 0.11 wt%, the dn/dT of Al2O3–water nanofluids is the same as that of water, in agreement with the results of Yousefi et al. [32].

Following the experimental measurement of nanofluid thermal conductivity and stability, the natural convection heat transfer of dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids with concentrations below 0.23 wt% (0.06 vol%) is investigated in this study. Additionally, a reliable temperature coefficient of refractive index (dn/dT) reported by Yousefi et al. [32] and validated in our previous study [11] is employed. Three nanofluid samples, each prepared using a different method, are tested to evaluate the influence of preparation technique and nanofluid concentration on thermal performance. Experiments are conducted in a 6 mL cavity with insulated sidewalls and isothermal boundaries at the top and bottom, where heating is applied from below and cooling from above. Compared to the high-volume setups commonly used in previous studies [14,15,18,19,22–27], the compact design of this model minimizes nanofluid consumption, making it more material-efficient and cost-effective.

The initial intent was to study a horizontal fluid layer (θ = 0°). However, in preliminary experiments it was observed that the thermal plume rising off the lower surface was unsteady. Also, the local convective heat transfer distributions on the hot and cold walls were highly sensitive to even slight deviations from perfectly horizonal. For these reasons, measurements were made with the cavity inclined at 9.3° from the horizontal. This constant inclination produces a steady counterclockwise recirculation with highly reproduceable local Nusselt number distributions. The inclined configuration also promoted faster thermal stabilization, allowing steady-state conditions to be reached within 1 h, significantly sooner than earlier studies [14,15,18,23,26]. A faster achievement of steady state is crucial for two reasons: longer testing times may introduce errors due to drift in the interferometer optical settings, and unstable nanofluids tend to become more non-uniform over time, leading to higher temperature measurement errors [8,9].

Another improvement in this study is the use of a shorter optical path length along the beam direction. An optical length of 10 mm is employed, significantly shorter than the 60 mm used in earlier interferometric investigations [23–25,27]. As demonstrated in the authors’ previous study [9], a shorter optical path minimizes temperature measurement errors associated with nanofluid instability. Additionally, previous interferometric studies with longer optical paths were limited to small temperature differences (ΔT = 0.5–3 °C) [22–26], as higher ΔT values caused excessive fringe compression near the heated surfaces. Even with small temperature differences applied, the resulting closely spaced fringes, particularly near the heated walls, made temperature measurement challenging, necessitating researchers to rely on curve fitting techniques. Moreover, using very low temperature differences, especially 0.5 °C, can significantly increase temperature measurement errors. However, reducing the optical path length in this study enables the application of higher temperature differences across the cavity, i.e., 10 °C, while still maintaining clearly distinguishable fringes near the heated surfaces.

As previously noted, most past interferometric studies of natural convection in nanofluids have reported unusually high heat transfer enhancements, even for extremely low particle concentrations. After implementing the improvements to the interferometric measurement technique discussed above, the main objective of the current study is to determine if these anomalous heat transfer enhancements persist.

Fig. 1 presents images of the experimental model used in this study. The model is displayed along the optical beam path in Fig. 1a. It consists of a middle fluid chamber positioned between two optical windows (λ/20 flatness) and is bounded at each end by Delrin walls to minimize heat dissipation. Fig. 1b illustrates the configuration of the middle fluid chamber, which contains Delrin plates on the lateral sides of the cavity to reduce heat loss and aluminum plates positioned at the top and bottom surfaces to conduct heat transfer. The cavity has dimensions of 30.0 mm in width, 20.0 mm in height, and 10.0 mm along the optical path, corresponding to a total volume of 6 ml. The test fluid is injected through one of the designated holes on the sides of the middle fluid chamber section shown in Fig. 1b. Temperatures at the top and bottom aluminum plates are monitored using 40-gauge T-type thermocouples, calibrated to an accuracy of ±0.2 °C, as illustrated in Fig. 1b.

The experimental model is positioned on a leveling mechanism and fixed between two heat exchangers located within the Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI). Using the leveling mechanism, the model is tilted by 9.3
°
to facilitate achieving a steady-state condition. This angle was determined after the model was fixed in place (i.e. a posteriori) by measuring the angle of the shadow of a plumb bob in the scale image. Subsequently, the model is heated from below and cooled from above using aluminum plates connected to temperature-controlled water baths, resulting in a 10 °C temperature difference across the cavity to induce natural convection.

As noted in the introduction, a short optical length has several key advantages for optical temperature measurements in nanofluids, such as greatly reducing the errors caused by concentration-induced fringes. However, this choice involves an important trade-off. The optical windows impart a viscous drag on the flow, making the flow field less two-dimensional. Also, the optical windows are not perfect insulators. As the optical length is reduced, the heat transfer to and from the optical windows will have more effect on the main free convective flow. For this reason, in the current study, separate interferograms were taken to measure the heat transfer rates from the cold and hot walls. To minimize the effect of heat exchange with the surroundings, for the hot wall heat transfer measurements, the lower hot wall was set to the room (ambient) temperature (23 °C) and the upper cold wall was set to 13 °C. Similarly, when measurements were made on the cold wall, the upper surface was set to 23 °C and lower hot surface was set to 33 °C. In all experiments, a temperature difference of 10 °C was maintained across the enclosure. This was done to maintain the total number of interference fringes constant. Because fluid properties are evaluated at the average surface temperature, there are slightly different Rayleigh numbers for each surface measurement.

In this study, a Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) is used, which employs a 15 mW He-Ne laser with a wavelength of
λ 0
= 632.8 nm as the light source. In the MZI setup, interference fringe patterns result from variations in the refractive index of the fluid, caused by changes in temperature or concentration. In a uniform stable nanofluid with no concentration gradients, changes in the fringe patterns are attributed solely to temperature variations. Therefore, testing nanofluid uniformity and stability is essential before performing heat transfer experiments. To visualize thermal fields, this study utilizes infinite fringe mode, where the reference and test beams are recombined in a parallel configuration. Further details of the MZI setup are available in the literature [33].

In natural convection within a cavity, the local Nusselt number (Nu) is obtained by:
(1)
N u =
h H
k
a v g
Where h, H, and
k
a v g
represent the local convective heat transfer coefficient, the characteristic length (H = 20 mm in this study), and the thermal conductivity of the fluid at the average temperature of the hot and cold surfaces, respectively. The local convective heat transfer coefficient is obtained from Fourier's law as:
(2)
h = −
k t
(
d T
d y
)

d T / d y
)

, and
k t
are the hot surface temperature, cold surface temperature, temperature gradient at the target surface, and fluid thermal conductivity at the target surface temperature, respectively. The target surface is either the top or bottom wall, both maintained at room temperature (23 °C), to examine nanofluid thermal performance at each surface. The target surface temperature gradient is attained by:
(3)

denote the laser vacuum wavelength, the optical length of the model along the beam direction (1 cm in this study), the temperature coefficient of refractive index at the target surface temperature, and the fringe shift gradient at the target surface, respectively. According to Yousefi et al. [32], the temperature coefficient of refractive index (dn/dT) for Al2O3–water nanofluids with concentrations below 1 wt% (
∼
0.3 vol%) is almost identical to that of pure water. This observation was also validated in our previous study [11]. Accordingly, in the present work, the dn/dT values for all nanofluids are approximated using those of pure water, as reported by Abbate et al. [34], which are valid in the temperature range of −3 to 80 °C.

The fringe shift gradient at the target surface is approximated by the gradient of the two adjacent constructive or destructive fringes (
Δ ε = 1
) closest to the surface, as:
(4)
(

d y
)

=

i
Where
Δ y
,
Δ y
i
, and SF are fringe spacing (m), fringe spacing in the image (pixels), and the image scale factor (pixels/m), respectively.
Δ y
i
is determined by analyzing the interference fringe patterns using digital image processing software developed in MATLAB. The scale factor is determined by measuring the number of pixels between two features on the image at a known distance. Small pins in one of the side walls were used for this purpose. Employing Eqs. (1)–(4), the local Nusselt number is:
(5)
N u = −

)

The average Nusselt number is calculated using the trapezoidal integration method applied to the local Nusselt number distribution along the heated surface.

Furthermore, the Rayleigh number is evaluated as:
(6)
R a =
g β
(
T h
−
T c
)
H 3
ρ 2
c p
μ
k
a v g
Where g,
β
,
ρ
,
c p
,
μ
are gravitational acceleration, volumetric thermal expansion coefficient, density, specific heat capacity, and dynamic viscosity, respectively. All fluid properties are determined at the average temperature of the hot and cold surfaces.

In this paper, three nanofluid samples with different preparation methods and concentrations are used to compare their heat transfer performance. The first nanofluid sample is produced by dispersing a known mass of Al2O3 nanoparticles (Sigma-Aldrich, primary diameter
d p
= 13
n m
,
ρ p
= 4000
k g /
m 3
) into deionized water to obtain 250 mL of Al2O3–water nanofluid at the concentration of 0.05 wt%. The sample is then sonicated for 1 h using a 750 W ultrasonic processor operating at 80 % capacity, delivering an output power of 600 W, with a cycle of 5 s on and 5 s off. This method is referred to as the direct method throughout the text. As presented in the authors’ prior work [11], increasing nanofluid concentration reduces nanofluid transparency, in part due to the presence of larger agglomerated particles. Therefore, to produce transparent nanofluids at higher concentrations, sedimentation and centrifugation techniques are employed.

In both methods, 250 mL of high-concentration nanofluid is prepared using ultrasonication at 600 W output with 5-s on/off cycles for 1 h. In the sedimentation method, the sample is left undisturbed for 24 h to allow larger particles to settle. In the centrifugation method, the sample is centrifuged at relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 1500 g for 90 min to separate out larger agglomerated particles. Subsequently, both samples are sonicated again for 30 min at 600 W with 5-s on/off cycles to ensure uniformity. Then, 50 g of each sample is placed on a hot plate for concentration measurement. Further details on the nanofluid preparation process are available in the authors’ earlier publications [8,9,11–13].

Nanoparticle size distribution and zeta potential are measured using the Zetasizer Ultra (Malvern Panalytical) instrument [35]. The volume-based hydrodynamic diameter distribution of different nanofluid samples is shown in Fig. 2 . Additionally, the complete specifications of each nanofluid sample are summarized in Table 1 . The pH of the nanofluid samples, measured with a digital pH meter (±0.02 accuracy), decreased with increasing nanofluid concentration, ranging from 7.02 to 5.80.

As illustrated in Fig. 2, nanofluids prepared using both the direct and sedimentation methods exhibit polydisperse distributions with some particle sizes reaching the micrometer scale. However, the sedimented sample demonstrates reduced polydispersity and a smaller average particle size, indicating that this method is more effective than the direct method. In contrast, the centrifuged nanofluid sample displays a monodisperse profile with the smallest average particle size of 64.5 nm, indicating that centrifugation effectively eliminates larger agglomerates, which are the main contributors to nanofluid instability [8]. This suggests that the centrifuged sample is the most stable among all nanofluids. Additionally, the higher zeta potential of the centrifuged sample further supports its superior stability, as it reflects stronger electrostatic repulsion between nanoparticles. Previous interferometric investigations conducted by the authors have also confirmed the enhanced stability of the centrifuged nanofluid using an interferometric method [8,12,13].

The density, specific heat capacity, and volumetric thermal expansion coefficient of the working nanofluids are determined based on mixing theory as follows:
(7)

n f

ϕ v

ϕ v
)

Where
ϕ v
is the nanofluid volume fraction, and the subscripts nf, bf, and p denote nanofluid, base fluid, and particle, respectively. The applicability of the above mixing theory equations was experimentally demonstrated by Ho et al. [18] for Al2O3–water nanofluids at concentrations below 4 vol%. Furthermore, the viscosity of the working nanofluids is calculated using the correlation proposed by Maïga and Nguyen [36], given as:
(10)
μ
n f
μ
b f
= 1 + 7.3
ϕ v
+ 123
ϕ v
2
In this study, nanofluid concentrations are expressed as weight fraction (
ϕ w
) to avoid introducing uncertainties associated with nanoparticle and base fluid densities in the measurements. However, to apply Eqs. (7)–(10), the weight fractions must be converted to volume fractions using the following equation:
(11)
ϕ v
=
1
1 +
(
1 −
ϕ w
ϕ w
)
(
ρ p
ρ
b f
)

The Al2O3 nanopowder used in this study has the following thermophysical properties:
ρ = 4000
k g / m
3,
c p
= 765
J / k g · K
,
β = 2.5 × 10
−5 1/K. Using the above equations, it was found that the changes in density, specific heat capacity, volumetric thermal expansion coefficient, and viscosity for all three nanofluids were less than 0.5 % relative to the values for water, with the maximum change in viscosity reaching 0.42 % at 0.23 wt% compared to water. Therefore, the density, specific heat capacity, volumetric thermal expansion coefficient, and viscosity of water, based on Touloukian's data [37], are used for the nanofluids in this study.

The thermophysical properties determined in this study differ significantly from those reported by Rao et al. [23], who reported substantial variations in density, specific heat capacity, and volumetric thermal expansion coefficient based on mixing theory. For 0.01 to 0.04 vol% Al2O3–water nanofluid, their study reported a maximum deviation at 0.04 vol%, showing 11.5 % increase in density, 11.4 % decrease in specific heat capacity, and 14.8 % decrease in volumetric thermal expansion coefficient compared to water. In contrast, our calculations using mixing theory indicate that these properties remain almost identical to those of pure water at such low concentrations (0.01–0.04 vol%). This discrepancy may have arisen from a misinterpretation in their calculations, where a concentration of 4 vol% may have been mistakenly used instead of 0.04 vol%. Furthermore, Rao et al. [23] applied the Sharma correlation [31] to calculate viscosity and reported an 18 % reduction in the viscosity of 0.01–0.04 vol% Al2O3–water nanofluid compared to that of pure water. However, our calculation using the same correlation [31] indicates only a slight reduction of approximately 1 %. These miscalculations might have led to significant errors in the calculation of the Rayleigh number in their study.

Since various factors such as synthesis method, particle properties, additives, and pH influence the thermal conductivity of nanofluids, its direct measurement becomes essential [11,30]. Therefore, in this study, the thermal conductivity of the working nanofluids is experimentally measured using the Thermtest MP-2 hot wire device with an accuracy and repeatability of ±5 % and ±2 %, respectively. Details on the use of this instrument can be found in the authors’ previous publication [11]. Using this instrument, it was found that the thermal conductivity of all three nanofluid samples, measured at 23 °C (room temperature), 18 °C, and 28 °C (average temperatures), was the same as that of water within the uncertainty range of the device. These findings align with previous interferometric results [10,11], confirming that the thermal conductivity of dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids is almost the same as that of water. Furthermore, this agrees with well-established mathematical models, such as the Maxwell [38] and Hamilton-Crosser equations [39], which predict almost no enhancement in thermal conductivity for dilute nanofluids with concentrations below 0.23 wt%. Consequently, the thermal conductivity of water, as reported by Touloukian et al. [37], is applied to all nanofluid samples in this study.

The uncertainty in the local Nusselt number is evaluated using the method proposed by Kline and McClintock [40], considering a 95 % confidence interval, as follows:
(12)
δ
(
N u
)
N u
=
(
δ
( H )
H
)
2
+
(
δ
( L )
L
)

+
(
δ
(
T h
−
T c
)
T h
−
T c
)
2
+
(
δ
(
d n
d T
)
d n
d T
)
2
+
(
δ
(
S F
)
S F
)
2
+
(
δ
(
Δ y
i
)
Δ y
i
)
2

The uncertainty in the laser vacuum wavelength is negligible and omitted. Similarly, the uncertainties in the fluid thermal conductivities at the target surface and average temperature has been neglected. The uncertainties of each variable and their impact on the local Nusselt number uncertainty for water, when the target surface is the bottom surface, are given in Table 2 .

As shown in Table 2, the uncertainty for water with the bottom surface as the target is ±4.4 %. The uncertainty increases when the top surface is the target, due to the formation of an undesired bubble, which will be further explained in later sections. Additionally, the 0.16 wt% nanofluid, which is the least transparent fluid in this study, has the highest uncertainty of ±8.6 %. Thus, the uncertainty in this study ranges from ±4.4 % to ±8.6 %. Additionally, as can be seen in Table 2, the main source of uncertainty in this study is the vertical fringe distance measurement (
Δ y
i
), which stems from optical imperfections that produce noise in the measured fringe spacing.

In the first stage of the study, deionized water is injected into the cavity to establish the baseline performance as well as the reproducibility of the results. The injection process sometimes leads to the formation of air bubbles inside the cavity, with the largest one typically appearing near the upper corner, having an average diameter of approximately 10 % of the target surface length or the model width (Dbubble/W = 0.1). Since air bubble formation is sometimes unavoidable, particularly during nanofluid injection, its impact was assessed. Moreover, preliminary experiments were also conducted by setting the bottom and top surface temperatures to 28 °C and 18 °C, respectively, with the average temperature of 23 °C (room temperature). The results showed that an energy balance was not achieved at the top and bottom surfaces due to heat loss or gain, which is attributed to the short optical path length of the model. To minimize this effect, subsequent experiments are designed with either the top or bottom surface maintained at room temperature (23 °C) to reduce heat exchange and measurement errors.

Based on this adjustment, two experimental configurations are examined. In the first set of experiments, the bottom surface is maintained at room temperature (23 °C), while the top surface is cooled to 13 °C to induce natural convection with a temperature difference of 10 °C. This configuration results in an average temperature and Rayleigh number of 18 °C and 9.7
×
105. To demonstrate repeatability and assess the effect of bubble formation, three independent experimental runs, referred to as cases c1, c2, and c3, were conducted under identical conditions. The corresponding interference fringe patterns at the beginning and after 1 h, upon reaching steady-state, are illustrated in Fig. 3 .

In the second set of experiments, the top surface is kept at room temperature (23 °C), whereas the bottom surface is heated to 33 °C, maintaining the same temperature difference of 10 °C. This setup produces an average temperature of 28 °C, and Rayleigh number of 1.8
×
106. The corresponding interference fringe patterns at the beginning and after 1 h, once steady-state is reached, for three independent runs (h1, h2, and h3) conducted under the same conditions are illustrated in Fig. 4 .

As shown in Figs. 3 and 4, bubble formation is more pronounced when the top surface is the target surface, since the bubble tends to rise to this surface. Specifically, in case h1, a visible bubble is present at the top surface, whereas in cases h2 and h3, the top surface is almost free of bubbles, allowing the investigation of the bubble's impact on the top target surface. In contrast, when the bottom surface is considered as the target surface (cases c1, c2, and c3), since the bubble forms at the top surface, it does not interfere with the measurement region. Therefore, three repeated runs were carried out in this configuration to confirm the repeatability of results and to ensure that bubble presence in non-target regions does not significantly influence the measurements.

The uniform illumination in the interferograms shown in Fig. 3a and 4a shows the high quality of the infinite fringe setting with the absence of temperature and concentration gradients at the initial point. For brevity, only the initial interferogram of one variation in each case is presented. After applying a 10 °C temperature difference from bottom to top and reaching a steady-state condition, the resulting infinite fringe patterns (Fig. 3b–d and 4b–d) illustrate the temperature fields within the domain. The Nusselt number was determined by measuring the vertical fringe spacing in the interferograms and applying Eq. (5), as described in the methodology section. Fig. 5 shows the local Nusselt number distribution along the x-direction for all variations in both cases. Moreover, the average Nusselt number for three variations of each case, along with the data from the empirical correlation of Catton [41], is presented in Table 3 . It is important to note that the local Nusselt number cannot be directly measured at the Delrin side wall. Instead, measurements are taken at the closest possible point, and linear extrapolation is applied to estimate the value at the surface. This limitation is especially evident in case h1, where a large bubble at the target surface prevents measurements near the wall.

As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the local Nusselt number distributions for the three experiments in the case where the bottom surface is the target surface are generally the same within the uncertainty range of the study. In contrast, Fig. 5b shows that when the top surface is the target surface, slight variations appear in the local Nusselt number within a small region affected by the air bubble, between the bubble case (h1) and the bubble-free cases (h2 and h3). However, outside of the bubble region (main larger region), all three cases exhibit similar local Nusselt number distributions within the uncertainty range. The localized variation caused by the bubble has a negligible impact on the average Nusselt number, as the values for the bubble and bubble-free conditions are almost identical, as presented in Table 3. Furthermore, the average Nusselt number results for both configurations—bottom surface as the target (c1, c2, and c3) and top surface as the target (h1, h2, and h3)—show good agreement with an empirical correlation for inclined enclosures in the literature [41].

Before conducting natural convection experiments on nanofluids, it is essential to evaluate their stability. As illustrated in Fig. 2 and Table 1, despite having the lowest concentration (0.05 wt%), this sample exhibits the highest degree of polydispersity and an average nanoparticle size of 906.9 nm by volume, as no sedimentation or centrifugation was performed to remove larger particles. The 0.16 wt% sample, prepared using sedimentation, also displays polydispersity but with a lower average nanoparticle size of 589.4 nm. In contrast, the 0.23 wt% sample, produced by centrifugation, is the only monodispersed case, containing significantly smaller nanoparticles with an average size of 64.5 nm. Additionally, this sample exhibits a higher zeta potential compared to the other two, indicating better stability.

The enhanced stability of the 0.23 wt% sample compared to the other two is further supported by previous research conducted by the authors using MZI [8]. This technique has the advantage of visualizing and quantifying nanofluid uniformity across the entire nanofluid domain. Accordingly, prior studies [8,12,13] reported that in an unstable quiescent nanofluid, concentration gradients differ at the top and bottom surfaces, resulting in different undesired concentration-induced fringes near each surface. These fringes tend to be more pronounced near the top surface. In the case of the 0.05 and 0.16 wt% samples, additional fringes are visible only at the top surface. However, due to the relatively large nanoparticle size in these samples, the bottom surface exhibits only a thin sedimentation layer with no noticeable additional fringes. Conversely, while the 0.23 wt% sample shows less distinct concentration-induced fringes, they appear on both the top and bottom surfaces, showing lower intensity at the bottom. More details can be found in the prior studies [8,12,13].

The primary reason for investigating natural convection heat transfer at both the top and bottom surfaces at the room temperature of 23 °C is to examine how different possible concentration gradients affect the temperature field and heat transfer measurements.

Well-sonicated, uniform nanofluids are injected into the experimental model cavity in separate experiments. Initially, since the nanofluids are uniformly distributed and no temperature gradient is applied, the interferograms exhibit uniform illumination, similar to water, as depicted in Fig. 3a and 4a. For brevity, these interferograms are not shown. After applying a temperature difference of 10 °C from bottom to top for 1 h, interferograms for all three nanofluid samples, under both low and high Rayleigh number conditions (where either the bottom or top surface is the target), are presented in Fig. 6 . The local and average Nusselt numbers along the x-direction for all cases, along with the water results for comparison, are illustrated in Fig. 7 and summarized in Table 4 .

As shown in Fig. 6, the 0.23 wt% centrifuged nanofluid sample exhibits higher image contrast than the other two samples, indicating that centrifugation effectively removes large particles—light scatter from the larger particles is the primary contributor to reduced image clarity. In contrast, the 0.16 wt% nanofluid sample has the least clear image, leading to higher experimental uncertainty. Further details can be found in our previous publication [12].

As illustrated in Fig. 7, the findings indicate that within the measurement uncertainty of the study, the local and average Nusselt numbers for all nanofluid samples are the same as those of deionized water. Given that the thermal conductivity of all nanofluid samples is equivalent to that of deionized water, the convective heat transfer coefficient and overall heat transfer rate are also the same. Consequently, for dilute Al2O3–water nanofluids with concentrations below 0.23 wt%, the Nusselt number, convective heat transfer coefficient, and heat transfer rate exhibit no measurable deviation from those of water. Additionally, the method used to prepare the nanofluids does not affect the natural convection heat transfer results. It should be noted that the current experimental setup had a short optical length, and hence a low sensitivity to concentration gradient errors. So, this is not a conclusive result. For other experimental designs—particularly those with longer optical paths, as seen in the literature [23–25,27]—errors due to concentration gradients can be significant.

Despite differences in stability among the nanofluid samples in a conduction-dominated environment [8,12,13], the observation that all nanofluids exhibit the same Nusselt number as water suggests that the mixing induced by natural convection is sufficiently strong to counteract instability and maintain uniformity, as least for the current experimental design. This aligns with the findings of Buongiorno et al. [42], who suggested that in the presence of turbulent eddies, turbulent transport of nanoparticles dominates over other nanoparticle transport mechanisms. If the mixing were insufficient to homogenize the nanofluid, the refractive index in the upward (y) direction would increase with decreasing temperature, while simultaneously decreasing as the concentration reduces. In such a scenario, the opposing effects of temperature-induced fringes (Δε
T ) and concentration-induced fringes (Δε
ϕw ) would diminish the fringe formation near the target surface, resulting in smaller fringe shifts and lower heat transfer rates. It is important to note that the nanofluid used in this study is highly dilute (
≤ 0.06
vol .
%
), minimizing concerns related to concentration gradients. However, such errors may become significant at higher concentrations, as reported by Khalili et al. [43] in the case on 1 vol% Al2O3–water nanofluid.

The findings of this study align with those of Ho et al. [18], who observed no enhancement in the Nusselt number and convective coefficient of the Al2O3–water nanofluid at a concentration of 0.1 vol% (0.4 wt%) and Rayleigh numbers around 106. However, it contrasts with previous interferometric studies that reported significant heat transfer enhancement in dilute nanofluids with concentrations of less than 0.04 vol% (0.16 wt%) [22–27]. Although these studies did not scientifically assess nanofluid stability, the present analysis suggests that concentration gradients in an unstable nanofluid, if they exist, can introduce errors that lead to a reduction in heat transfer rather than an enhancement. On the other hand, the misleading enhanced thermal conductivity [23,24,27] and reduced temperature coefficient of refractive index (dn/dT) [23,25] reported in these optical studies could have contributed to the high heat transfer enhancements observed.

Beyond the factors discussed above, the discrepancy could also stem from the use of a longer optical path length in previous studies (6 cm compared to 1 cm in the present study). A longer optical path results in densely packed fringes near the target surface, making it difficult to accurately distinguish and measure their temperature. Additionally, some fringes may be obscured due to refraction effects at the surface caused by the large optical path length. To overcome these challenges, previous researchers applied curve-fitting techniques to extract fringe temperatures. This method could have introduced errors in fringe temperature measurements. Furthermore, to reduce the number of fringes, they [22–27] used very small temperature differences, ranging from 0.5 °C to 3 °C. For instance, in this study, thermocouples were calibrated with an accuracy of ±0.2 °C, meaning that errors in temperature measurements could reach as high as 80 % if the temperature difference is 0.5 °C.

In this study, a short optical path length of 1 cm was chosen for several key advantages. This shorter path length results in fewer fringes, making the ones near the target surface distinguishable. It also enables the application of higher temperature differences (10 °C), which reduce the errors in the temperature difference measurements. Moreover, it reduces the concentration-induced fringes resulting from instability, especially when compared to the increased number of temperature-induced fringes that arise at higher temperature differences, thereby decreasing the possible errors in heat transfer measurements.

A drawback of using a shorter optical path length (1 cm) is that it increases heat loss from the optics. To mitigate this, the target surface is kept at room temperature (23 °C) to minimize heat loss to the ambient. The agreement between the water results and the literature demonstrates that there is no significant heat loss from the target surface affecting the heat transfer experiments, provided the target surface is kept at room temperature. In contrast, the other surface, whether colder (13 °C) or hotter (33 °C) depending on the case, is subject to heat gain or loss to the ambient, introducing potential errors. As a result, an energy balance between the two surfaces is not achieved, and the surface that is not at ambient temperature must not be considered as the target surface for heat transfer analysis.

As a side note, thermophoresis, where nanoparticles migrate from the hot (bottom) surface to the cold (top) surface, could play a role in offsetting sedimentation-induced concentration gradients. The combined effect of natural convection mixing and thermophoresis may contribute to the uniformity of the nanofluid, preventing concentration gradients from introducing errors in heat transfer measurements. However, since thermophoresis is relatively weak [42], particularly at low concentrations where it induces a lower thermophoretic diffusion mass flux, natural convection becomes the dominant mixing mechanism. Further investigation is needed to separately analyze the effects of these two mechanisms, which remains a topic for future research.

This study investigated the effects of adding Al2O3 nanoparticles to water on natural convection heat transfer within a cavity using laser interferometry. Al2O3–water nanofluids at concentrations of 0.05, 0.16, and 0.23 wt%, prepared using different methods, were examined. These low concentrations were selected due to the unusually high heat transfer enhancements reported in the literature using optical methods at such concentrations [22–27]. Mach–Zehnder interferometry (MZI) was employed for its ability to visualize and quantify both temperature and concentration fields. In previous studies by the authors [8,11], the stability of the nanofluid without a temperature gradient was assessed, and its thermal conductivity was measured in a conduction-dominated environment using MZI. Accurate evaluation of both nanofluid stability and thermal conductivity is essential for determining heat transfer characteristics in interferometric studies. With improvements in interferometric measurement technique, the results revealed that at concentrations below 0.23 wt%, the Nusselt number, convective coefficient, and total heat transfer rate show no noticeable enhancement compared to pure water, indicating that adding Al2O3 nanoparticles at such concentrations does not improve thermal performance.

In this study, a shorter optical path length was used in the experimental model, representing an improvement in optical heat transfer measurement technique. This approach resulted in more distinguishable fringes near the target surface, as they were less densely packed and less affected by surface refraction. Additionally, the shorter path length allowed for the use of higher temperature differences than those reported in the literature [23–25,27], reducing temperature measurement errors. In cases of unstable nanofluids, concentration-induced fringes caused by instability were less pronounced than temperature-induced fringes from the applied temperature gradient. The current measurements indicate that this allows natural convection mixing to become strong enough to counteract instability, thereby maintaining nanofluid stability and avoiding temperature measurement errors due to concentration gradients. One limitation is that, due to heat loss from the optical windows for the short optical path setup, accurate analysis of nanofluid heat transfer behavior was restricted to room temperature, where heat loss is minimal.

Soheil Sahamifar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. David Naylor: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jacob Friedman: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: David Naylor reports financial support was provided by Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada is gratefully acknowledged. This work was funded via Discovery Grant RGPIN-2020-04327.
